sentences.text: May.
Yes.
We were very worried, because the Russians, they-- they were very hungry for women.
And they didn't dare to sleep with the German women that they encountered for fear that they may stab them in the back.
So they were very happy to see the Jewish girls, because they says, we are brothers!
We are brothers.
Anyway, nothing happened, thanks God.
And they gave us a wagon with two-- with-- with four rubber wheels-- big, big wagon-- and a horse.
And we-- there was-- there was something different in our camp than anyplace else.
There were still the last transports from Hungary.
It was so close to the end of the war that they-- they-- some women got in who were pregnant, and who were not detected that they are pregnant.
And then when we were already in the Sudeten, where there we no Vernichtungslager, no extermination camps, they didn't know what to do with it.

And they felt by then that it will be-- that it will be a good thing if they keep these women with the babies, because they will be able to say that how good they were that they saved women with children.
So to sit out to our camp, they emptied two rooms, and they brought a few Jewish women.
They gave them beautiful lingerie, and they kept them very well.
And they had their babies there.
So after we were liberated, there was one woman in particular who stayed with us with a baby.
So on the wagon, we piled this woman and my mother.
And some girls, they collected by then a little-- some sweaters, some stuff, you know.
They went to the Germans, and they took it from them.
And they put it on the-- on this wagon.
And for the girls, the Russians gave bicycles, because they-- they captured the whole battalion or so, Germans who had-- were on bicycles.
And they brought them in here.
They made out of this camp a prison camp.
And they gave us bicycles.
And we started out to go home.
The-- the terrain there is very hilly-- up and down-- mountains and beautiful woods.
And the girls were bicycling.
And the one was driving the-- the wagon, this-- this wagon.
And the wagon had brakes that you could put on.
But they didn't work.
And once when the wagon was coming down the hill, the horse tripped.
And he hit his head on a-- on a mark-- on a stone that marked the road, and dropped dead.
And the-- the wagon hit the horse and stopped.
So we unhitched the horse and hitched ourselves to the wagon, and put the bicycles--
[LAUGHS] --on the top of the wagon.
And we pulled it to the village.
We pulled it to the village.
And a farmer took us in and gave us a room in a barn-- on the hay-- that we should sleep there.
And we went every day to the railroad tracks to see if there is going to be a train.
Finally, a train came.
And we got on it.
And we came all the way to Moravska Ostrava.
This is in-- this is on the way home.
And we couldn't go any further, because the bridges were bombed, and there were no roads.
Because the Germans, when they were retreating, they had a-- a-- a tractor plow the road.
So they went by, but the people who would becoming after them, the road became narrow, and they couldn't go through.
So we stayed there, again, about three weeks in Moravska Ostrava.
There, I met a cousin.
And we met some other people who were also stuck.
They were on their way home.
They got stuck there.
Then one day, it came a thing that there is a truck.
If we want, there is room.
We can go.
Sure enough, we got on the truck.
We went to another town where they unloaded us.
There, they gave us a horse and a wagon.
And we got on that and went further, till we came to a train.
And there, we waited all night.
And the train came.
And-- and we got on the train.
And we were coming home.
Now, all through this time that we were away from home, we didn't-- no matter who we met, we never dared to ask whether they saw my father.
Because we were so worried that he's not alive.

My father, being a physician, he-- he-- we couldn't even imagine that he should be able to wash a shirt, or wash a pair of socks, or keep himself clean, or he certainly wouldn't push himself that he should-- he should get something extra to eat.
What he is going to do?
And so this is-- was a beautiful, beautiful surprise, that when we arrived home, and were starting off the-- the train, my father was there.
And he looked wonderful!
And so we were again reunited in my-- in our hometown.
And by then, my brother was also home.
And he got married in between.
And so we all-- we didn't go to-- to the house-- to our house, because the Russians occupied it.
In the-- in my father's waiting room, beautiful waiting room, they had horses, because it was a huge room.
And we stayed in another Jewish home with my brother and sister-in-law.
Now, the-- we were under Russian rule.
And again, my father was very worried, because in Poland, the Russians were very mean to-- or cruel to the people who were wealthy.
They-- they took them to Siberia.
They separated families.
They-- they by all means, they wanted to prevent that they should be again be able to be reached.
They gave them a passport.
And they couldn't settle 40 kilometers from where they to used to live.
And they couldn't stay longer than two years in any given place.
And my brother, he had a run-in with the-- with the Russians also.
And he said that he's leaving.
He's going to Czechoslovakia.
Now, my father was worried that if he-- his son goes away, and he loves his son, and they will close the borders, because he didn't trust the Russians.
Then we will be stuck, and we won't be able to get out.
And so we arranged for a smuggler to-- to take us over the border once again, without-- not taking anything with us, not even clothing or anything.
Because we didn't want to-- if they-- if they would stop us, we didn't want to look suspicious.
This way, my mother put a kerchief on her head, and she had a basket on her arm.
And she would-- we-- we decided that she will say that we were in the-- on the market in town, and we are going home.
And we came to a-- a-- a village.
We were in a-- in a horse barn.
We waited for the night to come.
And we were 10 people.
And that man is going to cross us over the border.
Now, this is flat terrain.
This was from-- from Russia to Czechoslovakia-- to Slovakia, actually.
And-- and-- and from there we will go to Prague.
That's where my brother was.
Now, the guy told us that if we see flares go up, we should right away-- because he was afraid from the border patrol.
We should right away flatten ourselves on the ground.
And you should be very careful.
So everybody was going like this, you know?
And you had almost eyes in the back of your head, because you were watching so.
In the morning, we were supposed to arrive to a gypsy's house across the border.
The guide only came with us to the border, and he left.
We came to the gypsy.
The man wasn't home.
We had to go someplace else.
But anyway, my mother had some money.
And she asked, is there somebody Jewish here in town?
And there was a Jewish man.
And he told-- mother said to come over.
And she told him what is it about.
And she hired again a wagon.
And they took us to-- to [NON-ENGLISH] No.
What was it?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know if I'm saying that right.
So they took us to a-- a town, a big town.
This was in Slovakia.
And if you know the-- the Czechoslovakia Republic was Czechi, Moravia, Slovensko, and Podkarpatska Rus.
And we are from this Podkarpatska Rus area.
So to work our way back to Prague, you had-- we-- we smuggled ourselves to Slovakia, and then Moravia and Czech.
So there, a Jewish family took us in.
And the next day, they bought us train tickets.
And they told us they cannot go with us, but we should follow them, and they will show us where the train station is.
And we got on the train.
And we came to Prague.
We came to Prague, and hardly were we there two days, and they-- they-- they told us that there will a big search.
And the people who are without a-- a passport or without the permission, who came from Podkarpatska Rus, they will be all taken back by the Russians.
And we got scared.
And there was a chance to go further.
And we got on a bus that carried children to France.
And we arrived to Paris.
All.
No, just-- just my mother, my father, and me.
My brother stayed in Prague.
And-- and we arrived to Paris.
And there, my parents-- we had some relatives.
And we ate there.
And there was a couple also who came to eat there.
And these people-- this was a lawyer.
And he told my mother that they are in midst of buying a home for orphans who are coming from Poland, and would she go and advise them whether the house that they saw is good or not.
Then mother--
Yes.
And my mother went.
And these people liked my mother's-- my mother.
And they hired us, that mother should supervise the kitchen.
And father should supervise the schooling of the children.
And I shall be responsible for the-- that they should be clean, and they should-- I went with them for walks, et cetera.
And this house was in [NON-ENGLISH].
That is about 60 kilometers from Paris.
And it's a very, very small, sleepy town.
And there was a big house with a gorgeous, gorgeous chestnut tree, and a big yard, and the garden.
And they brought about 24 children there.
The youngest--
This was in '40-- this was-- we-- we got there '46.
This was '46.
And-- and actually, you know, like in '45, the war was over.
In the fall, we got home.
It was-- it was fall.
And early spring, we already left, because all this.
So many things happened, but it didn't take such a long time.
And-- and these children, the youngest-- there were three that were about five year old.
And the oldest was 12-year-old.
And they were-- I-- it was wonderful, because I spoke fluently Polish.
Because when we were in Galicia, I learned how to speak Polish.
And I grew their hair-- the little girls.
All, they all had bows and little braids.
And Friday night-- Friday afternoon, I dressed them beautifully.
And we sang songs.
And we prayed with them.
And my father learned with them.
And-- and it was-- it was wonderful.
Now these children were supported by an organization that's called Rescue Children.
This-- this organization started after the second world war.
And for $5,000, you could be a foster parent to a child.
And Eddie Cantor, then in the Magazine and in the newspapers, Eddie Cantor only had daughters.
And he was the foster father of a little boy in this home.
And they--
Yes.
And then some of the Americans, they adopted some of these children also.
And I keep in touch with some.
The rest, they went to Israel.
And some children were old enough and smart enough that they only wanted to go to Israel, because my father advertised in a German newspaper.
Actually, this newspaper is an-- an American newspaper but in German language.
The name is And he-- he advertise-- and this goes all over the world-- and he advertised in this newspaper for-- for relatives for these children.
And some found relatives, but they didn't want to go to them, because the only way they could go to them is if they denied that they are Jewish.
And they didn't want to go.
Because some of these-- see, Argentina or Brazil, they didn't accept Jews after the second world war.
So the-- the relatives wrote that we send you papers, but don't write that you are Jewish.
And these children, in particular, two cousins were-- they-- they survived in a convent.
And the Jews had trouble.
This organization who brought them-- the Americans gave the money to the French organization.
And the French organization sent people to Poland to get these children out.
And some had trouble getting out.
And some children, they were so convinced, you know-- they barely knew that they are Jewish.
And anyway, they didn't want to go through this again.
And then we stayed in France for about a year and a half.
And-- or-- this was early '46, late '47.
So it's almost just-- yeah, about a year and a half.
Because in December '47, we got our visas.
And we left France.
And we came to America.
We came to America.
And we met here the family.
My brother was already here already by then.
He was in California.
And my father had a sister in New York.
And my mother had a half-brother in Detroit and a brother in California.
And so it took about two months, because everybody wanted to treat us and everybody wanted to see us.
And we came to California to find out that in California, the only way you can take your boards, if you are a citizen.
So my father didn't want to wait five years.
And we went back to New York.
We went back to New York, and my mother--
No.
My father was an intern in the Bellevue Hospital.
And my mother was a cook in a Jewish high school.
And I was also a cook in a Jewish high school.
She cooked on the meat kitchen, and I cooked on the dairy kitchen.
And we lived in Williamsburg.
And Williamsburg, to me, was paradise.
I met such wonderful people-- poor people-- compared, you know, that how they lived and how I could observe.
But they were so wonderful.
And they were so religious.
But religious in a sense-- in Europe, if you were very, very Orthodox or Hasidic, you had no fun if you were young.
You couldn't do anything.
You couldn't participate in anything that other children do-- plays, or-- or-- or games, or nothing.
And I came to America.
And I met a family.
I-- somebody who adopted two-- two children from this home in [NON-ENGLISH] said that if I come to America, he will give me a job if I need a job.
He had a resort, an orthodox, beautiful resort.
And when I came, and I needed work, I called him up.
And he gladly hired me.
And I met there some other young people who worked in the resort in the summer.
Some were students.
Some were not.
But they were all Jewish.
And when I came to the city and worked as a cook, this one girl, an American girl, she looked me up.
And she took me under her wings.
And she treated me that I just can't tell you.
And because of her, I became even more religious than I-- or observant.
Because religious I was, but more observant than I was at home.
And we had such a wonderful time together.
We are friends still today.
And they took me.
And when my father came home from the hospital, then I had nowhere to sleep, because I slept in the same bed with my mother.
We only had a-- a little room.
And they took me in.
If I was sick, her mother took care on me.
And they were just wonderful.
And because I had her as a friend, I learn much faster English.
By the way, my mother spoke fluently English.
And my-- she taught my father in Europe yet how to speak English.
And I heard English all the time in the house, but I didn't know how to speak.
And-- and this family, as I say, they had a tremendous influence on me.
And-- and I found life in America wonderful-- Williamsburg, with the-- all the-- the-- the through and through Jewish.
And to hear on the bus, the-- the black or the Gentile bus driver say, gesundheit, and to hear on the radio or in nightclubs Jewish words, but they were-- it-- it was something unbelievable.
And then I got a letter one day.
And I met a man who was our former landlord.
And he says, oh, it's a good thing I see you.
A letter came for you, and I didn't know where you moved.
And I didn't know how to give this to you.
And he pulls out of his pocket a crumpled up old letter.
And he gives it to me.
And I take it home.
And I read it.
And I say to my mother, a man is writing to me a letter.
I know who he is.
But why is he writing me a letter?
I mean, we have nothing-- we-- what is this?
So I'm very straight.
And I sit down.
And I write a letter to him.
And I said, it's wonderful that you are inquiring that how we are, and what is this is, and all that.
But what gave you the idea to write to me in the first place?
And he answers the letter with a letter, I want to marry you.
And when my mother heard this, she says--
She says, you are engaged.
Well, I did see him, but I wouldn't know whether he's short or tall, or whether he has blue eyes, or brown eyes.
So I didn't know him, because--
See, he's from the same area that I am.
And we-- my cousin and I, we went through once his village.
And then, everybody was anxious to know who came home, or what.
And we met him.
But I had older cousins.
And I thought that, you know, I'm just the little one, you know.
I'm not interested in boys.
Well, make a long story short, my mother, she knew his father and mother.
And everything she liked about them.
And she says, if-- you are engaged.
And he lived in South America.
And he couldn't come to America, because he was registered for a visa.
And people like that didn't get a transit-- they didn't get a visitor's visa.
So my parents decided that I should go to Chile.
And I went to South America.
And we got married.
And two years later, we came home.
And we had a little girl.
And we lived happily ever after.
[LAUGHTER] Really, God was good to me.
And he's a wonderful, wonderful person.
No.
But see, it is really sometimes enough that we came from the same background.
We had the same background, the same language, the same-- see, after we got engaged, because he sent me the wedding band in the letter, we started to correspond.
And we found out that we are so alike, that really, sometimes I'm scared that they are so alike.
And we feel-- like if he hurts, I hurt, because we feel the same way.
And-- and it really-- everything worked out wonderful.
And it's not important that somebody should be tall, or somebody should be very masculine-looking.
It's what kind of a person.
And somebody should be bashert for you.
You know, it should be meant for you.
And so it worked out really wonderful.
And we are married 47 years, thanks God.
And we hope that we'll be married yet many years.
And we have three beautiful children, two daughters and a son.
And they-- they-- we all live-- I mean, our daughter, our youngest daughter just got married.
And she lives in Israel.
And we live with a-- we have a great love for Israel.
We spend lots and lots of time working for Israel through an organization.
Shelters for Israel.
For Israel.
It's not bomb shelters that we build-- houses-- housing.
And we were already, since the Six-Day War-- that's when we went the first time-- 20 times in Israel.
And it's really a marvelous-- we-- we love it.
And now our youngest daughter got married to an Israeli young man.
And they make their home in Israel.
Her name is Susie Serenevits-- her-- her married name.
And our oldest daughter, she's also a pharmacist.
She got married when she was 19.
And she has four beautiful children.
And she lives across the street from us.
Her name is Rosie Silverstein.
And her husband is Ron.
And they have-- Michal is the oldest-- Josh, Benjamin, and Joseph-- the four children.
And our son, if I may say-- and I don't know if it means a lot to somebody else, but to us it means a lot-- when he was a young man, it was the time of the-- of the Vietnam War.
And he's a pacifist, a total pacifist.
And he opposed it.
And he was against it.
And he was rebelling against everything that had to do with the government.
And he didn't live at home.
And I worried.
I worried only that he shouldn't be brainwashed and he shouldn't want to be something else than a Jew.
And he was a very good boy.
And he worried about everybody who didn't have-- or who had to go to war.
And he lives in Santa Barbara.
And there are not too many Jews there.
And I worried.
He's very handsome.
And he's very good, that he should-- he should stay Jewish.
And my mother passed away when she was 91.
My mother and father, they both lived in Los Angeles.
She passed away when she was 91.
And when I was sitting shiva, Allen our son, came home and was with me.
And before he went away-- went back to Santa Barbara, he says, what can I do?
Can I do something for you, mother?
And I asked him, the only thing what I would ask you, that you should learn how to pray for an [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, for-- the [NON-ENGLISH] is where a person stands when he prays with ten people.
And he went back to Santa Barbara.
And there is a Lubavitch rabbi.
And he went to him and said, Rabbi, I want to learn.
And from that day on, he became orthodox.
He didn't travel on Shabbos.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] He slept by the rabbi on the floor.
And he didn't eat [NON-ENGLISH].
And today, he has a beard, a beautiful wife who has a sheitel.
And they have two children.
And he's so good.
He doesn't have make a big living.
He just started to finally, because he wouldn't do anything that-- that is against the Sabbath or against the religion.
But if he has two, one he will give away.
And in his house, anybody who needs to sleep or stay over, they can come to his house.
There is room for everybody, whether it's a family of nine or whether it's a single, there is room for everybody.
And his wife is just the same.
And they have two beautiful children.
And this makes us very happy.
And I always pray to-- you know-- I-- I'm-- well, this is how it is
Yes.
OK.
This is my great grandmother, Bertha Rosenstein Klein, born 1847.
In [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH]-- then it was Austria-Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my great-grandfather, Samuel Klein.
And this is also-- it was in [NON-ENGLISH].
And these two pictures were taken because a cousin who was-- or a nephew-- who was in America, came home.
And he said, you all have to take a picture, because the future generations should know how you look.
And he took these pictures.
A professional photographer took these pictures.
And when we came to America, this cousin gave us these pictures.
Because in Europe, all the pictures were-- almost all the pictures were lost.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my grandmother and grandfather.
Josipovits is their name-- Gizella Josipovits and Moritz Josipovits.
The grandmother's name is Klein-- Gizella Klein Josipovits.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She was born in [NON-ENGLISH].
And the date, I don't know.
And she lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
She was a wonderful grandmother-- perished in the Holocaust-- both.
